results.possibleIdentity: Bernice Dauman was an artist and printmaker active from at least the late 1960s through the 1990s, with a significant presence in the New York City art scene before moving to Arizona. She was a member of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), the influential non-profit organization founded by Billy Klüver and Robert Rauschenberg to foster collaborations between the arts and engineering. Her affiliation with E.A.T. is documented in the organization's membership directories from the late 1960s. In the 1970s, she was part of a circle of New York-based female artists and printmakers; her name appeared in the Fall 1977 issue of Womanart magazine in a list of artists associated with the Graphic Arts Center (also known as The Print Center), a non-profit graphic design and printmaking service then located at 464 Greenwich Street in Manhattan. Later in her career, she exhibited in Phoenix, Arizona, where she was featured in a 1994 group exhibition titled 'Connections: Personal and Environmental' at the 11 East Ashland gallery. Her work in this period was shown alongside that of Justine Mentor Clarysse and Jean Thomsen, focusing on themes of environmental and personal connectivity.
